title: Molecular Analysis of Bacterial Pathogens in Otitis Media With Effusion
abstract:
Molecular Analysis of Bacterial
Pathogens in Otitis Media With Effusion
J. Christopher Post, MD; Robert A. Preston, PhD; Jerome J. Aul; Margaret Larkins-Pettigrew, MD; Jill Rydquist-White;
Kenneth W. Anderson; Robert M. Wadowsky, ScD; David R. Reagan, MD, PhD; Elaine S. Walker, PhD;
Lawrence A. Kingsley, DrPH; Anthony E. Magit, MD; Garth D. Ehrlich, PhD
Objective.\p=m-\Todetermine if the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) can detect
bacterial DNA in pediatric middle ear effusions that are sterile by standard cultural
methods.
Design.\p=m-\Single-center,blinded, comparative study of diagnostic assays. The
PCR-based detection systems for Moraxella catarrhalis, Haemophilus influenzae,
and Streptococcus pneumoniae were designed and validated using a battery of
DNAs obtained from cultured bacteria. Chronic middle ear effusion specimens were
collected and comparatively analyzed by culture and the PCR.
Setting.\p=m-\Tertiarycare pediatric hospital.
Patients.\p=m-\Atotal of 97 middle ear effusions were collected from pediatric out-
patients at Children's Hospital of Pittsburgh (Pa) during myringotomy and tube
placement for chronic otitis media with effusion (duration >3 months). All patients
had failed multiple courses of antimicrobial therapy and were diagnosed by a com-
bination of validated otoscopy and tympanograms.
Main Outcome Measure.\p=m-\Differencesin the percentage of positive test results
between PCR-based assays and culture for M catarrhalis, H influenzae, and
S pneumoniae.
Results.\p=m-\Of the 97 specimens of otitis media with effusion, 28 (28.9%) tested
positive by both culture and PCR for M catarrhalis, H influenzae, or Spneumoniae.
An additional 47 specimens (48%) were PCR positive/culture negative for these
three bacterial species. Thus, 75 (77.3%) of the 97 specimens tested PCR positive
for one or more of the three test organisms. The minimum number of bacterial ge-
nomic equivalents present in the average culture-negative ear was estimated to be
greater than 104 based on dilutional experiments.
Conclusions.\p=m-\ThePCR-based assay systems can detect the presence of
bacterial DNA in a significant percentage of culturally sterile middle ear effusions.
While this finding is not proof of an active bacterial infectious process, the large
number of bacterial genomic equivalents present in the ears is suggestive of an
active process.
(JAMA. 1995;273:1598-1604)
From the Departments of Otolaryngology (Drs Post,
Larkins-Pettigrew, Magit, and Ehrlich), Pathology
(Drs Preston, Larkins-Pettigrew, Wadowsky, and Ehrlich,
Messrs Aul and Anderson, and Ms Pydquist-White), and
Infectious Diseases and Microbiology (Drs Wadowsky,
Kingsley, and Ehrlich), University of Pittsburgh (Pa);
Departments of Pediatric Otolaryngology (Drs Post,
Larkins-Pettigrew, Magit, and Ehrlich) and Pathology
(Dr Wadowsky), Children's Hospital of Pittsburgh (Pa);
and Department of Internal Medicine, James H. Quillen
College of Medicine, East Tennessee State University,
and Mountain Home Veterans Administration Medical
Center, Johnson City, Tenn (Drs Reagan and Walker).
Dr Post was a recipient of the Academy Research and
Training Award, American Academy of Otolaryngology\x=req-\
Head and Neck Surgery.
Reprint requests to Department of Pathology, Uni-
versity of Pittsburgh, 737 Scaife Hall, Pittsburgh, PA
15261 (Dr Ehrlich).
OTITIS media (OM) is the most com¬
mon primary diagnosis made by pedi¬
atrie practitioners in the United States
for children younger than 15 years.1
Acute OM is characterized by fever, pain
and constitutional symptoms, and abulg¬
ing tympanic membrane with decreased
movement during otoscopy and gener¬
ally resolves with antibiotic therapy.
Chronic otitis mediawith effusion (OME)
is characterized by the presence of fluid
in the middle ear cavity for 3 months or
longer with minimal constitutional symp¬
toms.2 Significant hearing loss resulting
from OME adversely affects speech and
language development in children.2 The
economic impact of OM is substantial;
more than $3.5 billion are spent annu¬
ally in the United States for the medical
and surgical treatment of this disease.3
Treatment for OM is controversial,4 and
OME has been the subject of a recently
published clinical practice guideline from
the Agency for Health Care Policy and
Research, Public Health Service, US De¬
partment of Health and Human Ser¬
vices.5
A limited understanding of the etiol¬
ogy and pathogenesis of OME has ham¬
pered the development ofrational thera¬
peutic approaches.2 The pathogenesis of
OME appears to be multifactorial, in¬
volving immature anatomy, initiating vi¬
ral infections that induce eustachian tube
dysfunction, retrograde movement of
bacteria from the oropharynx into the
middle ear cavity, and host physiologi¬
cal factors. Multiple infectious agents
have been implicated in OME, but a com¬
plete understanding of the pathogenic
cascade has been impeded by the inher¬
ent limitations of conventional cultural
methods.
Downloaded From: http://jama.jamanetwork.com/ by a Florida International University Medical Library User  on 05/26/2015
 The study of infectious diseases has
been profoundly altered by the advent
ofthe polymerase chain reaction (PCR),
an enzymatic in vitro DNA amplifica¬
tion process.6-11 The PCR has been pre¬
viously shown to detect bacterial DNA
in the absence of culturable organisms
in several human body fluids including
vitreous and synovial fluids.12'13
The central hypothesis of the present
study was that PCR-based analyses
would detect the presence of bacterial
DNA in culturally sterile middle ear ef¬
fusions. Therefore, PCR-based assays
were developed to detect Haemophilus
influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae,
and Moraxella catarrhalis DNA from
OME specimens, since these are the
three most commonly cultured bacteria
from the middle ear.14·16
METHODS
Patient Population
A total of97 middle ear effusions were
collected from pediatrie outpatients at
Children's Hospital of Pittsburgh (Pa)
duringmyringotomy and tube placement
for chronic OME (duration >3 months).
Patients ranged in age from 9 to 172
months, with a median age of26 months,
a mean of 42 months, and an SD of 38
months. All patients were diagnosed by
validated otoscopy and tympanometric
evaluation. Criteria for myringotomy
and tube placement included the pres¬
ence of middle ear fluid for at least 3
months despite multiple courses of an¬
timicrobial therapy, including agents ef¬
fective against ß-lactamase-producing
organisms. The PCR results obtained
as part of this study were not used to
influence the course of treatment. This
study was deemed exempt by the Hu¬
man Rights Committee of Children's
Hospital of Pittsburgh because only re¬
sidual clinical material was used for
analysis.
Clinical Specimen Acquisition
and Microbial Culture
The external ear canal was disinfected
and desquamated by placing 70% ethanol
in the ear canal for 1 minute. Immedi¬
ately following myringotomy, the effu¬
sion was suctioned from the middle ear
cleft with a sterile 5F suction-tip cath¬
eter attached to a Senturia trap. An ali¬
quot of the effusion was removed from
the trap for culture using a calcium algi-
nate swab on an aluminum shaft. All cul¬
tures were performed by the Clinical Mi¬
crobiological Laboratories of Children's
Hospital ofPittsburgh. Culture for M ca¬
tarrhalis was performed using blood and
chocolate agar media; culture for H in¬
fluenzae was performed using chocolate
agar medium. Culture for S pneumoniae
was performed using sheep's blood agar
and chocolate agar media. In addition,
tryptic soy broth cultures were inocu¬
lated with aliquots ofthe effusion, and all
cultures exhibiting growth were subcul-
tured on the agar media. Thioglycolate
tubes were also inoculated for selective
isolation ofanaerobic bacteria. Gram stain
and culture results were recorded for each
specimen. The remainder of the sample
was snapfrozen ondryiceandthentrans¬
ported to the University of Pittsburgh
Medical Center's Core PCR Facility,
where it was stored at -85°C before mo¬
lecular analyses.
Clinical Specimen Preparation
for the PCR
Following thawing of the effusion, a
   -µ   aliquot of the specimen was re¬
moved for further processing and the
remainder was refrozen. The specimen
was combined with 100 µ _ ofÑ-acetyl-
L-cysteine, prepared in sodium citrate
as described16 for clarification, vortexed
briefly, and allowed to sit at ambient
temperature for 15 minutes. After in¬
cubation, 1 mL of phosphate-buffered
saline (pH 8.0) was added, and the en¬
tire 1200 µ _ was subjected to sonication
at 55°C for 1 hour in a Branson water-
bath sonicator (Fisher Scientific, Pitts¬
burgh, Pa) to lyse the bacteria. Follow¬
ing lysis the cellular debris was pelleted
at 12 OOOgr for 15 minutes. The superna¬
tant was aliquoted and stored at -80°C
until use.
DNA Purification
To establish the sensitivity and speci¬
ficity ofthe PCR-based assays, pure bac¬
terial cultures were lysed and DNA ex¬
tracted using standard protocols.17
Polymerase Chain Reaction
The PCR-based assays were devel¬
oped for H influenzae, S pneumoniae,
and M catarrhalis. Primers and probes
for 'influenzae and Spneumoniae were
designed from on-line DNA sequence
information (GenBank) or were taken
directly from the literature.18,19 Mo-
raxella catarrhalis primer pairs were
developed from DNA sequence data gen¬
erated in our laboratory (R.A.P. and
E.S.W., unpublished observations, 1993).
The OLIGO primer analysis software
program (National Biosciences, Ply¬
mouth, Minn) was used as an aid in the
design of primers and probes. All prim¬
ers and probes were synthesized on site
using standard ß-cyanoethyl phosphora-
midite chemistry on an Applied Biosys¬
tems Ine (ABI) (Foster City, Calif) model
391 PCRMate DNA synthesizer. Oligo-
nucleotides were purified by reverse-
phase chromatography using OLIGO pu-
rification cartridges (ABI), vacuum cen-
trifuged to dryness, resuspended in IX
TE (10-mmoVL TRIS-HC1 [pH 7.5] and
1-mmol/L NaJEDTA), and quantitated
by ultraviolet absorption spectropho-
tometry at 260 nm.
Amplifications were performed in a
100-µ volume using the Perkin Elmer
system 9600 thermal cycler. The clinical
specimens were adjusted to a volume of
50 µL with water, and 50 µL of a 2X
master mix was added to the specimen.
Reaction conditions for all PCRs con¬
sisted of 50-mmol/L KC1, 10-mmol/L
TRIS-HC1 (pH 8.3 at 25°C), 2- to 2.5-
mmol/L MgCl2, 200 µ    /L of each of
the deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates
(deoxyadenosine triphosphate, deoxy-
cytidine triphosphate, deoxyguanosine
triphosphate, and deoxyuridine triphos¬
phate) (Pharmacia, Piscataway, NJ), 100
nmol/L of each primer, and 2 U of Taq
DNA polymerase (Perkin Elmer, Nor-
walk, Conn; Eastman Kodak, Roches¬
ter, NY). The reaction conditions for
each assay were optimized for anneal¬
ing temperature and for MgCl2 and
primer concentrations. This was accom¬
plished by performing serial dilution ex¬
periments of the DNA obtained from
pure cultures of the test organisms. Af¬
ter optimization of the PCR system for
a given primer pair, DNAs purified from
five to 10 clinical isolates of the same
species were evaluated to determine the
inclusivity of the assay. Finally, each
primer pair was evaluated for specific¬
ity against a battery of human patho¬
gens and commensal flora. All PCR
amplifications were performed in the
University of Pittsburgh's Core PCR
Facility, which was specificallydesigned
and staffed to eliminate false-positive
PCR results attributable to end-prod¬
uct
contamination.
The
Core
PCR
Facility serves as a clinical reference
laboratory and has been certified by the
College of American Pathologists for
molecular diagnostic testing.
To eliminate the possibility of bias,
strict criteria for the blinding of observ¬
ers and the interpretation of data were
followed. The individuals responsible for
the microbiological isolation and iden¬
tification ofthe bacteria were blinded to
the PCR results. All specimens analyzed
by the PCR were identified only by mul-
tidigit accession numbers, and the au-
toradiographs were interpreted by a cer¬
tified molecular diagnostician (G.D.E.)
who was blinded to the culture results.
As multiple PCR data points were gen¬
erated for each of the test organisms,
the final PCR result for each ofthe three
test species was determined indepen¬
dentlyby the application ofan algorithm,
without knowledge of the PCR results
for the other two test organisms.

the deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates
(deoxyadenosine triphosphate, deoxy-

triphosphate, and deoxyuridine triphos¬

plished by performing serial dilution ex¬

gens and commensal flora. All PCR

To eliminate the possibility of bias,
strict criteria for the blinding of observ¬
ers and the interpretation of data were
followed. The individuals responsible for
the microbiological isolation and iden¬
tification ofthe bacteria were blinded to
the PCR results. All specimens analyzed
by the PCR were identified only by mul-
tidigit accession numbers, and the au-
toradiographs were interpreted by a cer¬
tified molecular diagnostician (G.D.E.)
who was blinded to the culture results.
As multiple PCR data points were gen¬
erated for each of the test organisms,
the final PCR result for each ofthe three
test species was determined indepen¬
dentlyby the application ofan algorithm,
without knowledge of the PCR results
for the other two test organisms.
Labeling of Oligonucleotide Probes
The PCR products were assayed by
liquid hybridization of the amplified
DNA with a 32P end-labeled oligonucleo¬
tide probe that was complementary to
one ofthe strands ofamplified DNA and
located internally to both primers.20,21
Following PCR, a 30-µ _, aliquot of the
reaction was denatured by boiling in the
presence of 250 000 cpm of end-labeled
probe and 150-mmol/L NaCl and 2.5-
mmol/L Na2EDTA in a total volume of
50 µ ,. The reaction was then placed at
55°C for 10 to 15 minutes to allow an¬
nealing of the probe to the amplified
DNA. The hybridization products were
then analyzed by gel retardation analy¬
sis by subjecting them to electrophore-
sis through a 14-cm vertical 8% poly-
acrylamide gel for 60 to 90 minutes at
200 volts. Following electrophoresis, an
overnight autoradiographic exposure of
the gel was made on Kodak XAR-2 film
(Rochester, NY) to visualize the results.
The PCR results were compared to
standard culture results for M catarrha¬
lis, H influenzae, and S pneumoniae us¬
ing 97 pairs as the total sample. McNe-
mar's test, with correction for continuity,
was used throughout to test the null hy¬
pothesis of equivalent PCR and culture
results. Conservative two-tailed  val¬
ues are reported despite preliminary data,
which justify one-tailed values.
Diagnostic Assay for M catarrhalis
A limit-digest genomic library was
generated from M catarrhalis?2 and a
plasmid clone, designated M46, was iden¬
tified for further analyses. Character¬
ization of this clone by restriction en¬
zyme mapping revealed a 3.7-kilobase
insert, which was subjected to dideoxy
DNA sequence analysis (R.A.P. and
E.S.W., unpublished observations, 1993)
to obtain sufficient sequence data for
the design of M catarrhalis-speciñc
primers and probes. Two primer sets,
designated MCAT 1/2 and MCAT 51/52,
together with their respective internal
probes MCAT 3 and MCAT 53 (Table 1)
were designed using the OLIGO soft¬
ware package. These primer-probe sets
were demonstrated to support and de¬
tect amplification   M catarrhalis DNA
in a sensitive and specific manner. Us¬
ing liquid hybridization/gel retardation
analysis with the internal probes MCAT
3 and MCAT 53, respectively, it was
possible to routinely detect 10 fg (equiva¬
lent to approximately three genomic
equivalents) of purified M catarrhalis
Table 1.—Primers and Probes Used for the Specific Detection of Bacterial Pathogens in Otitis Media With
Moraxella catarrhalis Primers and Probes Designed From the M46 Clone

CGG TGC GTT GGG TTC AGA TGG CTT GTC AAT CAT TTG GTT
Haemophilus influenzae Primers and Probes Designed From the P6 Gene
GCA TAT TTA AAT GCA ACA CCA GCT GCT
CCA GCT TGG TCT CCA TAC TTA AC
GCA TTA GCG GCT TGT AGT TCC TCT AAC GAT GCT
Streptococcus pneumoniae Primers and Probes Designed From the PBP2B Gene

GAT GCC TGG CAT TAG TAT TCC TAC TTC TTG GGA

GCA AAT AAT GGT CGT GTG GCT CCT CGT ATT GTT GA
*Oligonucleotide primers and probes used to support polymerase chain reaction amplification and to detect
amplified products, respectively.
genomic DNA isolated from each of sev¬
eral clinical strains. However, no am¬
plification was detected in the presence
of 100 ng (in excess of30 million genomic
equivalents) ofDNA prepared from pure
cultures of a panel ofhighly related bac¬
terial species and strains including two
strains of Neisseria mucosa, Neisseria
lactamica, Neisseria flavescens, and
Neisseria sicca and five clinical isolates
ofNeisseria species. In addition, a panel
of DNAs purified from other human
pathogens and commensal flora were de¬
termined to be incapable of supporting
amplification. These bacteria included
Escherichia coli, Enterobacter cloacae,
Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter
species, Bordetellapertussis, Haemophi-
lus influenzae type B, Haemophilus in¬
fluenzae nontypable, Pseudomonas ae-
ruginosa, Borrelia burgdorferi, Strep¬
pyogenes, the viridans group of strep¬
tococci, Staphylococcus aureus, Staphy-
lococcus epidermidis, Enterococcusfae-
calis, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
The DNA isolated from human periph¬
eral blood mononuclear cells also did not
support amplification. In addition, no am¬
plification was evidenced from reagent
blanks containing no template DNA.
Analysis of Pediatrie Middle Ear
A comparative analysis for the detec¬
tion of M catarrhalis was performed in
97 chronic middle ear effusions by tra-
ditional microbiological culture methods
and PCR. Five (5%) of 97 clinical speci¬
mens tested positive by culture and
PCR. All the culture-positive specimens
tested PCR positive with both M ca-
tarrhalis-speciñc primer-probe sets. Us¬
ing our algorithm for PCR positivity, an
additional 40 specimens (41%) tested
positive for PCR only. Thus, the total
number of specimens testing positive
for M catarrhalis DNA was 45 (46.4%)
(Table 2). There was a highly significant
tendency for M catarrhalis culture-
negative specimens to test positive by
PCR (McNemar's test,  2=38.0, P<.001).
Ofthese PCR-positive/culture-negative
samples, 24 tested positive by both M
catarrhalis PCR primer-probe sets. Of
the remaining 16 PCR-positive samples,
10 tested positive by MCAT 1/2 and six
tested positive by MCAT 51/52.
An algorithm was developed to inter¬
pret the results of the PCR-based am¬
plifications. Negative controls were in¬
cluded for each set of assays, in general
one negative control was included for
each five clinical specimens, therefore
resulting in three negative controls per
gel. Ifany ofthe negative controls tested
positive, the entire run was discarded
To determine if a particular clinical
sample tested positive for the presence
ofM catarrhalis DNA, all samples were
assayed with each primer pair using 1,
5, and 10 µL of the sonicate. The results
were interpreted as follows: if a sample
Table 2.—Comparison of Culture and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) Results From 97 Pediatrie Middle Ear Effusions Analyzed for Moraxella catarrhalis,
Haemophilus influenzae, and Streptococcus pneumoniae
*1 df comparing total culture-positive specimens with total PCR-positive specimens.
tested positive at least once with each of
the primer pairs, it was considered posi¬
tive (in practice, only one sample was
called positive based on these minimal
criteria, and 75% of the specimens test¬
ing positive by both primer pairs were
positive for five or six of the six possible
data points). Samples testing positive
initially with one primer set and nega¬
tive with the other set were reanalyzed
in triplicate with the positive set. A
sample was considered PCR positive by
a single primer set if at least four of the
six datapoints for that primerpair tested
positive. These repeat PCR analyses
were performed by a different individual
who was unaware of the initial results.
Diagnostic Assay for H influenzae
Two PCR primer-probe sets from the
P6 gene   H influenzae were used. One
of these primer pairs, HI-IV/V, was
taken from the literature18 and used to
develop a PCR-liquid hybridization as¬
say with a corresponding internal oli-
gonucleotide probe, HI-VI. A second
primer pair, HIP61/62, and cognate
probe, HIP63, were designed and char¬
acterized for this study and demon¬
strated to sensitively and uniquely am¬
plify and detect H influenzae DNA.
These assays were characterized in a
manner similar to that used for M ca¬
tarrhalis. Sensitivity tests demonstrated
the ability ofboth these systems to rou¬
tinely detect 10 fg of H influenzae ge-
nomic DNA isolated from pure cul¬
three genomic molecules. Specificity
tests were performed using the test or¬
ganism panel (minus the Neisseria spe¬
cies) as for M catarrhalis, and amplifi¬
cation and detection were obtained only
with nontypable and type  strains of
Analysis of Pediatrie Middle Ear
The presence of  influenzae was
comparatively determined in the pedi¬
atrie OME specimen set by culture and
PCR as described herein forM catarrha¬
lis. A total of 21 (21.6%) of the 97 speci-
mens tested culture positive for H in¬
fluenzae. Each of the two primer-probe
sets identified 20 of these specimens as
H influenzae DNA positive. Together
the two sets achieved a 100% sensitivity
when compared with culture, since each
primer-probe set missed a different cul¬
ture-positive specimen. In addition to
the 21 specimens testingpositive by both
culture and PCR, an additional 32 (33%)
of the 97 specimens tested positive by
PCR only. Therefore, 53 (54.6%) of the
97 middle ear effusions had evidence of
H influenzae DNA. There was a highly
significant tendency for H influenzae
culture-negative specimens to test PCR
P<.001). Twelve of these 32 PCR-posi-
tive, culture-negative specimens tested
positive with both primer sets; 12 tested
positive with HI-IV/V only; and eight
tested positive only with the HIP61/62-
The algorithm used for determining
PCR positivity included the testing of
1-, 5-, 10-, and 20^L aliquote of the
sonicate prepared from the clarified
middle ear effusions with each of the
influenzae primer pairs. Therefore,
we obtained a minimum of eight data
points for each specimen. As with
M catarrhalis, a specimen was consid¬
ered positive if a positive signal was
obtained at least once with each primer
pair (in practice, none of the samples
were called positive by this criterion,
and only five specimens were positive
with four or fewer positive data points;
the majority ofthe double-positive speci¬
mens were positive for five or more of
the eight possible data points). When
the two primer-probe sets gave discor¬
dant results, one or more replicates of
the dilution series were performed for
the specimens in question using one or
both of the primer pairs. For a sample
to be considered PCR positive by a single
primer set, at least two of the four data
points corresponding to that primerpair
had to be positive if a single run was
used to make the decision. If multiple
runs were performed on a specimen with
a given primer pair, one positive result
per dilution set was required for a posi-
tive score for the specimen. In practice
all the specimens called positive for a
single primer pair had a minimum of
Diagnostic Assay for S pneumoniae
As for the other pathogens under
study, two primer-probe sets for Spneu¬
moniae were developed, JM201/202-203
and Spl/2-3 (Table 1). The Spi primer
sequence was taken from the literature,19
whereas all other primers and probes
were designed from published sequence
data corresponding to the PBP2B gene.19
Sensitivity determinations using puri¬
fied genomic DNA isolated from labo¬
ratory strains of S pneumoniae demon¬
strated the ability ofthese primer-probe
sets to detect reproducibly 100 fg of in¬
put DNA, equivalent to approximately
30 genomic equivalents, or an order of
magnitude less sensitive than the other
PCR-based assays. In several experi¬
ments, however, the JM201/202 primer
pair was able to detect 10 fg of input
greater sensitivity than that obtained
with the Spl/2 primer pair. Specificity
was documented using the same DNA
specimen set described herein for H in¬
Analysis of Middle Ear Effusions
Comparative analyses of the pediat¬
rie OME specimens were performed by
culture and PCR using the JM201/202-
204 primer/probe set for S pneumoniae.
Five (5.2%) of 97 specimens tested cul¬
ture positive and PCR positive for
S pneumoniae. There were no culture-
positive, PCR-negative specimens. An
additional 24 (24.7%) of the 97 speci¬
mens tested PCR positive. Our criteria
for positivity was essentially identical
to the H influenzae single primer-pair
algorithm. There was a highly signifi¬
cant tendency for Spneumoniae culture-
negative specimens to test positive for
PCR (McNemar's test,  2=22.0, <.001).
Of the 97 specimens, 19 were also
evaluated with Spl/2-3. The oneculture-
positive specimen evaluated with Spl/2-3
tested positive, and five of seven culture-
negative, JM201/202-positive samples
also tested positive with this second
Comparison of Culture and PCR for
the Detection of Bacterial Pathogens
in the Middle Ear and Determination
The percentage of specimens test¬
ing positive for at least one of the bac¬
teria (M catarrhalis, H influenzae, and
S pneumoniae) by culture alone and by
PCR is displayed in Table 2. A total of
28 (28.9%) of the 97 specimens tested
culture positive for one or more of the
three organisms under investigation. In
comparison, 75 (77.3%) of the 97 speci¬
mens tested PCR positive for one or
more of the three test organisms. None
of the culture-positive specimens were
PCR negative, whereas 47 of the PCR-
positive specimens were culture nega¬
tive for the three test species. Three
(10.7%) of the 28 culture-positive speci¬
mens tested positive for two ofthe three
study organisms. In contrast, 60 (80%)
ofthe 75 PCR-positive specimens tested
positive for two or three of the test
The observed increase in the incidence
of effusions testing positive for each of
the three test species by PCR over cul¬
ture was as follows: Mcatarrhalis, 892%;
Hinfluenzae, 253%; and Spneumoniae,
575%. The difference between the pro¬
portion of culture-positive and PCR-
positive specimens for all three organ¬
isms individually and collectively was
significant (P<.001) (Table 2). The ad¬
dition of the PCR data represents an
increase of 48.4% of the total specimen
M catarrhalis, H influenzae, and/or
S pneumoniae or a 268% increase in the
proportion of effusions testing positive
An additional 27 specimens supported
growth of other bacterial species, and
many of these supported growth of two
or more organisms including viridans
negative staphylococci, S aureus, Co-
rynebacterium species, Micrococcus spe¬
cies, and Enterococcus species. An ad¬
ditional six effusions evidenced rare
gram-positive cocci by gram staining.
A positive signal, following amplifi¬
cation and liquid hybridization, from a
l-µ aliquot ofthe sonicate is indicative
ofa minimum of36 000 bacterial genomes
being present in the initial effusion. This
calculation is based on the following data:
(1) a PCR/liquid hybridization detection
limit of three genomic equivalents; (2) a
1200-fold dilution of the specimen dur¬
ing the preparative process; (3) an ef¬
fusion volume of 1 cm3; and (4) the as¬
sumption that bacteria are distributed
in the middle ear in a Poisson distribu¬
The percentage ofculture-positive ef¬
fusions (28.9%) for the three target or¬
ganisms ofthis study is within the range
normally reported for children with
OME.23 All specimens were plated ac¬
cording to standard protocol on multiple
media and were subjected to broth en¬
richment cultures immediately after suc-
tioning the effusion in the operating
room. These measures were designed to
minimize any loss of bacterial viability.
All microbiological cultures were per¬
formed by experienced staff from the
Clinical Microbiology Department at the
Children's Hospital of Pittsburgh.
The PCR-based detection of H influ¬
enzae, S pneumoniae, and M catarrha¬
lis resulted in a 268% increase in the
number ofOME specimens testing posi¬
tive for one or more of these three test
species when compared with culture re¬
sults. This finding is in keeping with the
results of a comparative analysis of cul¬
ture and PCR in pediatrie cases of acute
OM in Finland, where it was found that
PCR for S pneumoniae alone increased
by 154% the number of specimens test¬
ing positive.24 Other methods of detec¬
tion have also suggested the presence of
bacteria in culture-negative effusions.
Positive gram stains were reported in
17% of 294 culture-negative chronic ef¬
fusion specimens,25 and assays for neur-
aminidase and endotoxin have suggested
the presence of S pneumoniae and
H influenzae, respectively, in sterile ef¬
fusions.26,27 Countercurrent immunoelec-
trophoresis has been used to detect an¬
tibodies to pneumococcal capsular poly-
saccharide in approximately one third
ofsterile effusions.28 Recent meta-analy-
ses have shown that a greater percent¬
age of cases of OME will resolve with
antimicrobial therapy when compared
with untreated controls, suggestingthat
at least some cases of OME are due to
Our demonstration of M catarrhalis
DNA in almost one half of the speci¬
mens strengthens the case that this or¬
ganism plays a role in OME.31 Although
it was possible using PCR to detect mul¬
tiple species in a majority of the effu-
sions examined, M catarrhalis was the
sole organism identified in six specimens;
this was equivalent to the number of
Spneumoniae-oiûy PCR-positive speci¬
The PCR data for H influenzae indi¬
cate that this known pathogen may play
a role in more than half of the cases of
OME. Recently, Hotomi et al32 described
the results of a PCR-based analysis of
patients with OME for H influenzae,
which confirmed our preliminary find¬
ings.33,34 In their study they found that
15 (55.5%) of the 27 effusions analyzed
from patients with OME tested positive
for H influenzae by PCR. This percent¬
age of PCR-positive effusions is nearly
identical to the positive rate (53 of 97 or
Do PCR-Positive, Culture-Negative
Results Reflect Persistence of DNA
An epidemiologica! study evaluating
molecular genetic dissimilarity
gested that bacteria do not persist in
the middle ear cleft between discrete
episodes of OME.35 Loos et al looked at
recurrent episodes ofHinfluenzae-me-
diated OME using restriction fragment-
length polymorphic analyses of paired
nasopharyngeal and middle ear isolates
from six children. In all cases the strain
Hinfluenzae recovered from the first
episode was genotypically distinct from
the strain of H influenzae recovered
from the second episode. This finding
suggested that the bacteria causing the
initial infection were cleared from the
middle ear cleft before another strain
a second infection, indicating
turnover ofthe infecting strain between
It is unlikely that the data reported in
the present study reflect the amplifica¬
tion of residual DNA from an earlier
episode of OM, because animal model
experiments clearly demonstrate that
purified bacterial DNA, coinoculated
with infectious organisms, does not per¬
sist for more than 2 days. Further, DNA
from pasteurized bacteria does not per¬
sist in an amplifiable form in the pres¬
ence of an effusion for more than 2 days
(G. D. Ehrlich et al, unpublished data,
1994). Extrapolating from this animal
model data, the detection of amplifiable
DNA in pediatrie OME specimens sug¬
gests that viable bacteria were present
in the middle ear, either at the time of
sampling or within days of sampling.
Pathogenesis and Culturability
The detection of bacterial genomic
DNAby PCR-based assays in this study
is not proof of an active bacterial infec¬
tion, therefore, its clinical significance is
currently unknown. These results, how¬
ever, are in accord with other reports
that have dealt with the limitations of
culture for the detection of bacterial
pathogens. Initially, PCR-based assays
were used for the elucidation of known
fastidious and unculturable bacterial
pathogens.36"40 The DNA amplification
strategies have recently been used to
detect the presence of genomic DNA
from nonfastidious bacterial species from
clinical specimens that did not support
The inability to successfully culture a
given bacterium may be due to one or
more factors. Previous antimicrobial
therapy may have a bacteriostatic ef¬
fect if the level ofdrug reaching the site
of infection is sublethal. In the current
study all patients had received multiple
courses ofantibiotics before undergoing
myringotomy and tympanocentesis. This
treatment may have resulted in a popu¬
lation of bacteria with reduced replica¬
tive vigor in vitro, but still capable of
eliciting an adverse effect on the host.
Alternatively, it is possible that not
all strains of a given pathogenic species
can be cultured using standard methods
regardless of previous antimicrobial
treatment. This concept of difficult cul-
turability has long been associated with
chronic bacterial diseases caused by
slow-growing pathogens.41 As a general
rule, slowly replicating pathogens, such
as those associated with chronic disease,
are the most difficult to culture and suc¬
cessfully treat with antibiotics. These
characteristics of poor antibiotic
sponse and lack ofculturability are most
likely related. The long replication cycle
means that the antimicrobial agents
must be present at their optimal levels
for a much greater period to be bacte¬
riocidal. It is possible that long-term
antimicrobial treatment ofpatients with
OME may select'for strains of common
pathogens (eg, S pneumoniae, M ca¬
tarrhalis, and H influenzae) that grow
more slowly and thus evade standard
antimicrobial therapy. Ifthis is the case,
it may explain in part the increased in¬
cidence of chronic OME that has been
observed during the last several decades.
Great effort was used to ensure that
the results ofthe culture and PCR-based
assays would accurately reflect the mi¬
lieu in the middle ear and that they would
not be compromised by external micro-
bial contamination or end-product con¬
tamination (carryover).42 To control for
the former, the external ear canal was
treated for 1 minute with 70% ethanol
before myringotomy. This procedure
renders the skin bacteria nonviable and
results in desquamation, which provides
for bacterial removal by suction before
myringotomy, thus reducing the chance
of introducing organisms or DNA from
the external environment into the middle
The norm for cultural results (applied
to this study) is to consider all speci¬
mens positive that yield any bacterial
growth. However, we used more strin¬
gent criteria for classifying a specimen
as PCR positive to minimize the chance
ofmaking any false-positive determina¬
tions. The algorithms used for determi¬
nation of PCR positivity for the three
test organisms varied slightly for the
following reasons. The specimen set was
initially analyzed for Hinfluenzae using
1-, 5-, 10-, and 20-µ aliquote. Analysis
ofthese data indicated that nò additional
20-µ aliquot, therefore the Mcatarrha¬
lis assays were performed without the
20-µ 1 aliquot. This difference in the
number of data points generated ac¬
counted for the difference between the
influenzae and M catarrhalis algo¬
rithms. The algorithm that was used for
determining S pneumoniae PCR posi¬
tivity reflected the fact that only one
primer pair was used to amplify S pneu¬
moniae DNA for the entire specimen
set, since the second primer pair (SP1-
2) did not meet our sensitivity criteria.
All the PCR-based analyses were per¬
formed in the Core PCR Facility, which
is the performance site for PCR-based
clinical diagnostics for the University of
Pittsburgh Medical Center.43 This facil¬
ity was specifically designed and staffed
to eliminate carryover contamination;
physical, procedural, and biochemical an¬
ticontamination measures were used for
isolated laboratories for specimen re¬
ceiving and processing, reaction prepa¬
ration, amplification and analysis, and
imaging, each with independent venti¬
lation systems. Reagent controls were
included in all experimental runs. Any
run registering a positive signal in any
of the reagent controls was discarded
and the entire run repeated with fresh
aliquote ofthe clinical specimens. In prac¬
tice, because of the anticontamination
measures in force, it was only necessary
to repeat a few assays of the thousands
conducted during a period of 2 years.
Therefore, the probability ofa false-posi¬
tive result is estimated to be less than
one per 1000. These stringent criteria
for calling a specimen positive by PCR
(coupled with a recognized PCR inhibi¬
tion problem caused by our sampling
methods) probably resulted in an un¬
derestimation of the percentage ofposi¬
Recently, we found that both compo¬
nents of a calcium alginate-tipped alu¬
minum swab inhibit the PCR amplifi¬
cation process.44 This type of swab was
used to inoculate the microbiological me¬
dia used in this study, resulting in the
introduction of inhibitors into the effu¬
sion that was used for PCR analysis.
This inhibition may explain some of the
differences observed between the two
sets ofprimers used for each of the bac¬
terial species under study. Inhibition was
readily apparent in many ofthe samples,
since the amplification signals at lower
specimen input volumes were stronger
than at higher input volumes. In some
cases the signal was completely missing
at the higher volumes but repeatedly
observable at lower input levels.
These data suggest but do not prove
thatbacteriamaybepresentin agreater
percentage ofOME specimens than pre¬
viously suspected. If further studies
show that PCR-positive results are in¬
dicative of an active infectious process,
this finding may affect antimicrobial
treatment regimens. These data may
also influence the bacterial species and
subtypes chosen for vaccine develop¬
ment, since vaccination targets are cur¬
rently determined by culture frequency.
